response: The global food system faces unprecedented pressure as the world population is projected to reach 9.7 billion by 2050, requiring a 60% increase in food production even as climate change reduces crop yields. Agriculture accounts for roughly 25% of greenhouse gas emissions, while water scarcity—highlighted by the overdrawn Ogallala Aquifer—and soil degradation on 33% of the world’s arable land further undermine productivity. Compounding these issues, about one-third of all food produced is lost or wasted between farm and fork. Proposed solutions span AI-driven precision agriculture, urban vertical farming, alternative proteins, drought-resistant crops, and regenerative practices, supported by policy shifts in subsidies, trade, and carbon pricing to scale up while keeping food affordable.
